page: Vultr voc-m-8c-64gb-1600s-amd is an Optimized Cloud Compute instance featuring 8 vCPUs powered by an AMD EPYC Milan processor clocked at 2.0 GHz. Built on the x86_64 architecture, this server allocates 8 physical cores with a single thread per core on a shared CPU model. It is equipped with 64.0 GB of system memory, yielding a high memory-to-core ratio of 8.0 GB per vCPU. Storage is bundled with a local 1600 GB NVMe SSD, while no dedicated GPU hardware is present.

Benchmark data indicates strong memory performance, with Passmark memory scores and large-block read/write memory bandwidth ranking in the top 25 percent. Single-core CPU performance via stress-ng is highly competitive, placing in the top 10 percent. However, multi-core CPU performance and Passmark CPU scores align with the middle 50 percent average, reflecting the shared CPU allocation tradeoff. Database operations, Redis SET performance, and static web serving throughput also perform at average tier levels.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, this instance offers a high density of memory and local NVMe storage relative to its shared CPU allocation, making it a viable option for memory-intensive workloads that do not require sustained, exclusive multi-core CPU utilization. The combination of 64 GB of RAM and a 1600 GB NVMe SSD makes this server well-suited for database hosting, caching layers, memory-mapped file processing, and general-purpose web serving with moderate computational demands.

Utilizing an x86_64 architecture with shared CPU allocation, it features 64.0 GB of RAM, providing a high 8.0 GB of memory per core, alongside a bundled 1600 GB NVMe SSD. While single-core CPU performance ranks in the top 10% according to stress-ng benchmarks, multi-core performance and database operations align with average tiers. Large-block memory bandwidth is strong, placing in the top 25%. This resource profile delivers high memory and storage density, making it a cost-effective choice for memory-heavy applications. It is suitable for database hosting, caching layers, and general-purpose web serving.
